claim: Test-driven development applies to LLM applications: write test cases and define evaluation criteria before writing prompts, then improve prompts until tests pass
actionable: Before iterating on any LLM prompt, build a test set with expected outputs and define passing criteria; only then optimize the prompt against those tests
source_title: Evaluating LLM-based chatbots: A framework for reliable AI assistants

claim: Good test sets must cover both happy paths (business-critical queries) and failure cases: edge cases, adversarial attacks, out-of-domain questions, rude inputs, PII leakage attempts
actionable: Design test sets with explicit coverage for: domain-specific questions, edge cases, adversarial prompts (jailbreaks, prompt injection), and sensitive topics relevant to your domain
source_title: Evaluating LLM-based chatbots: A framework for reliable AI assistants

claim: Extract test cases from production logs and real user interactions rather than synthetic examples; this ensures your evals reflect actual usage patterns
actionable: Never rely solely on synthetic test data; deploy early to collect real user questions, then update your test set based on actual usage patterns
source_title: Evaluating LLM-based chatbots: A framework for reliable AI assistants

claim: LLM-as-a-judge evaluation enables custom evaluation criteria beyond generic metrics, allowing you to measure what actually matters for your specific use case
actionable: Use LLM-as-a-judge for domain-specific evaluation criteria; write prompts that define 'good' in your business context rather than relying on pre-built metrics
source_title: Evaluating LLM-based chatbots: A framework for reliable AI assistants

claim: You must validate LLM-as-a-judge itself with human labels before trusting its evaluations; create a separate test set where humans label examples, then check if LLM judge aligns with human judgment
actionable: Before scaling LLM-as-a-judge to 100s of examples, validate it on 10-20 human-labeled examples; measure agreement rate between LLM judge and human labels
source_title: Evaluating LLM-based chatbots: A framework for reliable AI assistants

claim: For LLM-as-a-judge prompts, use binary scores, explicit explanations, examples, low temperature, and JSON output; these practices reduce hallucination and improve consistency
actionable: Structure LLM-as-a-judge prompts with: binary yes/no scoring, detailed criteria explanations, specific examples, temperature=0.0-0.3, and Pydantic-validated JSON output
source_title: Evaluating LLM-based chatbots: A framework for reliable AI assistants

claim: Evaluating chatbots requires assessing three layers: retrieval quality (RAG), response quality (relevance and correctness), and conversation structure (multi-turn flow and rules)
actionable: For RAG-based chatbots, build separate evals for: context retrieval precision/recall, hallucination checking, and conversation structure (e.g., transfer-to-human at right times)
source_title: Evaluating LLM-based chatbots: A framework for reliable AI assistants

claim: For RAG hallucination checking, compare retrieved context against LLM output to detect: contradictions, missing facts, or invented facts not supported by context
actionable: Implement hallucination checks that classify overlaps as: contradiction, missing facts, or hallucination; use entailment-based or Deep Eval's hallucination metric
source_title: Evaluating LLM-based chatbots: A framework for reliable AI assistants

claim: Conversation structure evaluation requires context of full conversation history, not just single turns; use LLM-as-a-judge with conversation examples to test multi-turn behavior
actionable: Evaluate conversation flows with test cases that include conversation history; check that agent behavior adapts contextually (e.g., different response for repeat 'transfer-to-human' request)
source_title: Evaluating LLM-based chatbots: A framework for reliable AI assistants

claim: Run LLM-as-a-judge evaluations 4-5 times due to non-determinism, then average the scores; don't trust a single evaluation result
actionable: Whenever using LLM-as-a-judge for scoring, run each example 4-5 times and average the results to account for LLM stochasticity
source_title: Evaluating LLM-based chatbots: A framework for reliable AI assistants

claim: Domain experts and subject matter experts should create and label test data, not engineers alone; SMEs understand what 'good' means in your business context
actionable: Involve your domain SMEs (support team, product managers, legal experts) in building test sets and labeling evaluation criteria; manual process is essential initially
source_title: Evaluating LLM-based chatbots: A framework for reliable AI assistants

claim: Use existing company standard operating procedures and guidelines as the foundation for eval criteria; convert human-facing SOP documents into chatbot eval rules
actionable: Extract your existing customer-facing SOP documents (greeting format, question order, closing language) and translate these directly into eval criteria and test cases
source_title: Evaluating LLM-based chatbots: A framework for reliable AI assistants
